text: Friends, this video demonstrates our technique of performing a laparoscopic cholecystectomy in an elderly lady who has underwent a previous laparotomy.
Dense bowel adhesions are visible, which the surgeon carefully releases. The gallbladder is then exposed, showing its appearance after the adhesions are removed.
It was an acute calculus cholecystitis presenting seventy-two hours later.
The surgeon punctures the gallbladder, as the wall is difficult to grasp, allowing for decompression and improved handling during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon suctions out infected bile that is visibly leaking from the punctured gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a tooth forceps to gently pull the gallbladder upward, attempting to improve exposure for further dissection.
The surgeon makes the first incision on the peritoneum near the gallbladder, gradually incising the peritoneum to delineate the gallbladder wall.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct near the gallbladder, maintaining incisions close to the gallbladder wall. The suction device is used to clear the field, revealing the cystic duct as it comes into view.
The surgeon uses a bipolar cautery device to achieve hemostasis, allowing for clearer identification of the dissection planes around the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to carefully dissect and remove the surrounding fat from the cystic duct, revealing the duct more clearly.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers to ensure the dissection remains well away from the common bile duct (CBD), using electrocautery only when confident of the distance from the CBD.

The surgeon uses a monopolar device to gradually dissect the fat covering the cystic duct in the Hartmann's pouch region, revealing the cystic duct structure and the low ridge of the gallbladder more clearly.
The surgeon ensures good hemostasis before carrying the incision on the anterior side of the gallbladder, maintaining careful control to avoid bleeding.
The surgeon attempts to identify the dissection plane towards the cystic plate and liver, while the assistant struggles to hold the gallbladder due to large impacted zones at Hartmann's pouch.
The surgeon attempts to apply traction to the gallbladder, but the inflamed and thickened wall tears, resulting in significant bleeding.

The surgeon secures hemostasis and uses the assisting hand to lift the gallbladder from below, improving traction and visibility. A suction device is employed for blunt dissection and to remove inflammatory fluid obstructing the visual field.
The surgeon identifies the border of the gallbladder and works closely to it, using short bursts of cautery to remove any visible strands of tissue.

The surgeon works close to the gallbladder, using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection with a suction cannula. Short bursts of cautery are applied to strands near the gallbladder, ensuring no major structures are affected.
The surgeon employs short bursts of cautery to dissect tissue parallel to the cystic artery and cystic duct, creating a window for clearer visualization of these structures.

The surgeon encircles the cystic duct, revealing a wide dissected window. Despite this, further dissection is performed to separate the lower border of the gallbladder from the liver bed, aiming to achieve a clear view of the two tubular structures entering the gallbladder from both anterior and posterior perspectives.

On the posterior side, the surgeon performs meticulous dissection to remove the peritoneum, revealing the doublet view necessary to ensure no accidental injury to the common bile duct (CBD). Fat and inflammatory fluid are visible falling away as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon exposes the gallbladder bed, clearly visualizing the cystic duct from the anterior side. The cystic duct is well dissected, and attention shifts to the cystic artery. As the surgeon attempts to delineate the friable artery, it unexpectedly tears, resulting in a sudden gush of blood and pulsatile bleeding.

The surgeon manages a gush of blood from a friable artery by suctioning the blood to maintain visibility. A gauze is used to compress the area, stopping the bleeding after five minutes. The cystic duct is milked, revealing only one structure entering the gallbladder, confirming a safe critical view without accidental dissection of the common bile duct. Two proximal clips are applied to secure the duct.

The surgeon begins dissecting the gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully identifying the gallbladder wall and staying close to it to avoid entering the liver parenchyma. The dissection is performed with precision to maintain the correct anatomical planes.
The surgeon works close to the gallbladder, expanding and opening up the tissue planes on all sides to ensure precise dissection without entering the liver parenchyma.

The surgeon encounters dense adhesions that resemble liver parenchyma but are not. They change the direction of dissection, focusing on separating the gallbladder from the liver bed in a different area. The peritoneum is incised to better identify and dissect along the anatomical planes.

The surgeon uses the assisting hand to apply traction near the fundus of the gallbladder, revealing its thick wall. Instead of grasping, the instrument is kept open to facilitate dissection. The surgeon continues to identify the precise plane for dissection to avoid damage to the liver bed.

The surgeon incises further into the gallbladder, realizing the plane is incorrect and entering the gallbladder itself. They attempt to correct the dissection plane by holding the incised gallbladder wall and carefully dissecting between the liver and gallbladder wall to identify the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to open the correct plane between the gallbladder and liver bed. The assistant pulls the gallbladder towards their port to facilitate traction, allowing the surgeon to identify any vessels and apply electrocautery as needed.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum and enters the correct dissection plane near the gallbladder, using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques. The dissection is performed slowly and meticulously to avoid excessive bleeding, allowing clear visualization of the tissue layers and small vessels. The gallbladder is progressively released, with only a few small fibrous strands remaining attached.

The surgeon rotates the gallbladder from the left to the right side, releasing the last few attachments near the fundus. Small strands are holding the gallbladder, and the identified plane allows for rapid dissection.

The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing the liver bed. A small wash is performed, and hemostasis is achieved using a bipolar device. The area where the cystic artery was clipped is visible. The gallbladder is placed into an endo bag for retrieval.
